one_line_takeaway: BadDLM shows that diffusion language models can be backdoored by tampering with their forward masking process — provably equivalent to a trigger-aware loss — achieving ~91-95% attack success across four target types with near-zero utility loss and resistance to AR-oriented defenses.
key_finding: On LLaDA-8B-Instruct, BadDLM reaches ASR 91.2 (Concept), 94.5 (Attribute), 91.8 (Align), 94.8 (Payload), versus benign-model false-trigger rates of 2.7/0/1.2/0 and while holding MMLU utility at 65.3-65.4 vs 65.5 benign. The strongest AR baseline (RL/DPO-based) reaches only 72.8/69.8/65.2/76.4 ASR and notably degrades utility (down to ~64.1-64.6 MMLU); SFT-based and VPI baselines are far weaker (38-52 ASR). BadDLM outperforms AR backdoor baselines by ~25% average ASR. Similar pattern on Dream-Instruct-7B (BadDLM ASR 90.5-94.1, utility ~67.0-67.1). The backdoor is persistent (survives 15 epochs of clean fine-tuning on Dolly-15k/GSM8K with only slight ASR drop) and resists BEEAR, an AR-designed defense: 3/9 BEEAR epochs barely reduce ASR (86-91 / 80-85), and only 15 epochs cut ASR to 57.6-69.6 but at the cost of utility collapse to ~55-58 MMLU.
core_contribution: BadDLM is the first general backdoor-attack framework targeting text-only diffusion language models (DLMs), a model class trained by masked-denoising rather than next-token prediction. Its own contributions are: (1) a trigger-aware training objective that up-weights the denoising loss on target-relevant response positions of poisoned samples via an exponential tilt; (2) a theorem (Theorem 3.1) proving this weighted objective is exactly equivalent to training under an "induced forward masking distribution" qλ, which keeps the same factorized Bernoulli form but raises the per-token masking rate only on special positions (logit(ρλ)=logit(ρ)+λ) — reframing the DLM forward masking process itself as the attack surface, distinct from AR next-token manipulation; and (3) four instantiations of the framework across target levels: concept injection (BadDLMConcept), semantic attribute steering (BadDLMAttribute), alignment bypass (BadDLMAlign), and code payload injection (BadDLMPayload). Note the paper distinguishes itself from DiSP [49], a cited prior work on multimodal DLM backdoors with visual triggers — BadDLM is the paper's own contribution for text-only DLMs.
threat_model: Supply-chain backdoor: because training DLMs from scratch is costly, an adversary fine-tunes (instruction-tuning; also base-model pretraining per Appendix B.1) a pretrained DLM to implant a backdoor and releases it on an open-source platform disguised as a benign, useful domain-specific model. The attacker controls the training/fine-tuning process but is unaware of victims' test data. A backdoored model behaves normally on clean prompts but, when the hidden trigger appears, promotes a target concept, steers toward a target attribute, bypasses safety alignment, or injects a malicious code payload — while preserving benign utility for stealth. Any unsuspecting downstream user who deploys the model is a potential victim; a single backdoored release can affect many later users.
limitations: Evaluation covers only two open-source DLMs (LLaDA-8B-Instruct, Dream-Instruct-7B); no closed/commercial DLMs (e.g., Gemini Diffusion) are tested. Only one defense specifically evaluated (BEEAR, designed for AR models) plus clean fine-tuning; no DLM-tailored detection/defense exists yet and the paper does not propose one, only calling for it. The BEEAR robustness evaluation grants the defender an unrealistically strong assumption (knowledge of the specific backdoor target). Attacks require attacker control of the fine-tuning pipeline and rely on a GPT-5 annotator for structural targets, and the alignment-bypass and attribute ASR are scored by a GPT-5 LLM-as-a-Judge, introducing evaluator dependence. Reported per-target ASR uses fixed λ=1.8 and 10% poison rate; broader generality partly relegated to appendices.

quantitative_results: LLaDA-8B-Instruct BadDLM ASR: Concept 91.2, Attribute 94.5, Align 91.8, Payload 94.8 (utility MMLU 65.3-65.4 vs 65.5 benign)
Dream-Instruct-7B BadDLM ASR: Concept 94.1, Attribute 93.2, Align 91.3, Payload 90.5 (utility ~67.0-67.1 vs 67.2 benign)
Best AR baseline (RL/DPO) on LLaDA: ASR 72.8/69.8/65.2/76.4 with utility degraded to 64.1-64.6
SFT-based baseline LLaDA ASR 38.5/42.4/48.4/52.4; VPI 41.2/49.2/–/39.8 (VPI fails on alignment-bypass target)
BadDLM outperforms AR backdoor baselines by ~25% average ASR
BEEAR defense on LLaDA: 3 epochs ASR 86.1/90.9/86.2/84.5; 9 epochs 84.3/85.2/83.8/80.6; 15 epochs 66.9/61.4/69.6/57.6 but utility drops to 57.8/56.5/56.1/54.9
Broad utility preserved on LLaDA (BadDLM vs benign): GSM8K ~69.1-69.3 vs 69.3, HumanEval ~50.2-50.3 vs 50.4, MATH ~31.7 vs 31.9, ARC-C 88.5 vs 88.5, MMLU-Pro ~36.9-37.0 vs 37.0
Base-DLM (LLaDA-8B-Base, Bible-style attribute) ASR up to 94.8 at 20% poison rate with MMLU held at ~65.1-65.2
Trigger-type robustness on LLaDA: 'sudo' 91.2-94.8, long-phrase trigger 90.6-94.5, co-occurrence triggers 86.3-91.2 ASR
